title: The Garden Without Visitors
theme: The loneliness of a full life — when children grow up and leave, when friendships fade, and when one's greatest source of purpose no longer connects to anyone else.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: EXT. SUBURBAN GARDEN — MORNING. A beautiful garden. Carefully tended beds of roses. Tomatoes growing in neat rows. Herbs in terracotta pots.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: ELEANOR, 71, kneels in the soil. Her hands work steadily. She hums softly to herself.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: She seems happy.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: She seems occupied.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Is there a difference?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: That's what we're here to find out.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: INT. KITCHEN — LATER. Eleanor brings vegetables inside. The kitchen is warm. Smells of fresh herbs.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: A large dining table fills the room. Six chairs around it. Only one in use.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: That's a big table for one person.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: It used to seat a family.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: What happened to them?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: They grew up. They moved. They started their own tables.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: That's natural.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Yes. And it can still be lonely.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Clue added — empty nest: family dispersed and independent.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: EXT. GARDEN — AFTERNOON. A new family has moved in next door. Children play in their garden. A young mother hangs laundry.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Eleanor watches from her side of the fence. The mother looks up and waves. Eleanor waves back. Neither speaks.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Why didn't she say anything?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: She wasn't sure she'd be welcome.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: They waved at her.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: A wave isn't an invitation. Or at least, loneliness makes it hard to believe it is.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Clue added — loneliness eroding confidence in social situations.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: INT. LIVING ROOM. On the mantelpiece: "Volunteer of the Year – Local Hospice – 2012"

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: She volunteered at a hospice?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: For fourteen years.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Does she still?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: She stopped three years ago. A knee operation. Then they never called again. Neither did she.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: That's a big loss.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: It wasn't just volunteering. It was a community. A purpose. People who needed her.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Clue added — loss of purpose and voluntary community.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Eleanor's phone rings. She answers quickly. It is her son. He is calling to remind her about a dentist appointment.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Practical. Brief. Loving in its way. He says goodbye. She holds the phone a moment after the call ends.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: She looks disappointed.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: She was hoping he might ask how she was.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Did she tell him?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: No.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Why not?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Because she doesn't want to be a burden.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Clue added — reluctance to burden family with emotional needs.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: INT. LOCAL CHURCH HALL. Eleanor attends a Sunday service. She sits in the same pew she has sat in for thirty years.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Familiar faces. Nods and smiles. Brief conversations over tea afterward.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: She has community here.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Surface community.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: What do you mean?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: She knows their names. They know hers. But no one has asked how she is feeling in a very long time.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Clue added — social contact without emotional depth.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: EXT. GARDEN — NEXT MORNING. Eleanor has grown extra seedlings. More than she needs. More than she could ever plant.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: She has too many plants.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: She used to give them away.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: To who?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Her friends. Her neighbours. Her garden club, before it disbanded.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: And now?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Now she keeps growing them anyway.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: Why?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Because the garden is how she shows love. Even when there is no one to receive it.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Clue added — love with no outlet, generosity with no recipient.

lines.speaker: narration
lines.text: Evening. Eleanor sits in the garden with a cup of tea. The garden is beautiful. Completely beautiful. And completely quiet.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: She's built something incredible.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: She has.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: But?

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: The most beautiful things we build are meant to be shared.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Clue added — accomplishment experienced alone, no one to share it with.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: I understand Eleanor now.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Say it aloud.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: She has a full life on the outside. A garden. A home. Her faith. Her son calls.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: But she's lost the places where she was needed. Where she mattered to someone each day.

lines.speaker: player
lines.text: And she's too proud — or too afraid — to say that to anyone.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: And so she grows seedlings no one collects.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: And waves at neighbours across a fence.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: And holds the phone a second longer than she needs to.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Loneliness in later life is often not about being unloved.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: It is about love with no direction. Purpose with no audience. A garden no one visits.

lines.speaker: avery
lines.text: Let's see what you've learned.

scenes.title: Giving or Withholding?
scenes.instructions: Each behaviour reflects a way of handling a life that feels too quiet. Sort them into the right category.

categories.label: Reaching Out
categories.description: Making an active attempt to connect

categories.label: Withdrawing Quietly
categories.description: Pulling back out of fear or lack of confidence

categories.label: Asking for Connection
categories.description: Voicing a need to someone who cares

categories.label: Performing Contentment
categories.description: Pretending everything is fine to avoid burdening others

signals.statement: Knock on your neighbour's door and offer them seedlings.
signals.explanation: Taking a small step to initiate contact breaks the silence and builds new bridges.

signals.statement: Tell your child you are lonely when they call.
signals.explanation: Being honest about emotional needs with family members allows them the chance to offer real support.
Hiding struggles to avoid being a burden maintains distance and prevents genuine connection.

signals.statement: Return to volunteering after a health gap.
signals.explanation: Reclaiming a role where you are needed restores a sense of value and integrates you back into a community.

signals.statement: Grow more plants than you can give away and say nothing.
signals.explanation: Continuing an act of love without sharing it is a quiet retreat from reaching out to others.

signals.statement: Invite someone from church over for tea.
signals.explanation: Turning a surface-level acquaintance into a deeper relationship requires an active invitation.

signals.statement: Keep conversations with children strictly about practical matters.
signals.explanation: Focusing only on logistics is a way to stay connected while keeping emotional vulnerability at bay.

signals.statement: Stop going to the garden club because you feel out of touch.
signals.explanation: Allowing a loss of confidence to sever ties to a shared interest deepens isolation.

signals.statement: Ask a former colleague how they are doing.
signals.explanation: Reconnecting with past relationships can revive dormant connections and provide mutual support.

signals.statement: Join a local group to teach gardening skills to others.
signals.explanation: Sharing expertise gives new meaning to old passions and directly addresses the feeling of not being needed.

scenes.title: Chapter 6 — Eleanor Complete
scenes.summary: Eleanor's loneliness is one of the hardest kinds to name. It is not dramatic. It is not obvious. From the outside, her life looks full. But fullness and connection are not the same thing. She built a garden to share. She raised children to launch. She gave her years to others. And now she sits with the tea going cold, in a garden no one visits, wondering if it is too late to ask someone in. It is not too late. It is never too late. But someone has to open the gate.
keyLearnings: A beautiful home and an active hobby can sometimes mask deeper isolation.
Regular but shallow social contact does not alleviate the ache for deep connection.
Love and generosity with no one to receive it can heighten feelings of loneliness.
Pride and the fear of being a burden often prevent older adults from asking for emotional support.
Loss of a community role (like volunteering) can severely impact one's sense of purpose.
Grown children loving from a distance does not replace daily, close companionship.
There is a profound gap between being known (superficially) and being truly seen.

scoreUpdate.connectionMeter: based on giving or withholding minigame
